food_subgroup: Steamed starchy dumpling with pork filling — sweet potato starch and rice flour wrapper with minced pork, bamboo shoot, and shiitake mushroom filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for ba-wan Taiwanese meat dumpling. Closest parent concept: FOODON:03315595 (dumpling).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for ba-wan. Closest parent: A039A (Composite dishes — dumplings/stuffed pastry).
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for ba-wan Taiwanese meat dumpling as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard ba-wan piece weighs approximately 180g, comprising a thick gelatinous sweet potato starch and rice flour wrapper (~100g) enclosing a savoury pork, bamboo shoot, and shiitake filling (~80g), served with sweet-savory fermented soybean sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — component ingredients (sweet potato starch, pork, bamboo shoots, shiitake mushroom) combined with FAO/INFOODS Asia-Pacific Food Composition Tables for Taiwanese traditional preparations
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for ba-wan as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for sweet potato starch (FDC ID 168482), ground pork (FDC ID 167903), bamboo shoots (FDC ID 170393), and dried shiitake mushroom (FDC ID 169243), weighted by approximate recipe proportions (wrapper ~56%, filling ~44%). FAO/INFOODS SEA tables used as secondary cross-check for Taiwanese traditional preparations. Values are consistent with the input record (confidence HIGH per original enrichment). Per-100g figures represent the composite dish as served.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI based on analogous starchy dumpling foods — sweet potato starch GI ~65 (Atkinson FS et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); gelatinous texture and mixed filling reduce effective GI to approximately 52
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for ba-wan exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~52 using the following analogous evidence: (1) Sweet potato starch GI ~65 (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283); (2) thick gelatinous starch matrix physically resists amylase access, reducing effective GI below raw starch GI; (3) bamboo shoot and shiitake mushroom filling contribute dietary fibre and protein that further blunt glycaemic response; (4) resistant starch (RS3) formation on cooling further lowers effective GI. GL per 180g serving = (52 × 25.0 × 1.8) / 100 = 23.4, rounded to 23.0. GI category: LOW (≤55). This is a defensible analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content — approximately 684mg per piece (380mg/100g × 1.8), primarily from soy-based filling and fermented soybean topping sauce
hypertension.guidance: One piece of ba-wan contributes approximately 684mg sodium (~30% of the 2,300mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association); individuals with hypertension should request reduced sauce or consume without the topping sauce, which can reduce sodium by an estimated 100–150mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycaemic load of 23 per piece (MEDIUM GL) with GI ~52 (LOW); sweet potato starch wrapper is rapidly digestible but thick gelatinous texture physically slows amylase access
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ba-wan has a low GI (~52) but a medium GL (23) per piece due to the large carbohydrate portion (45g per serving); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one piece per meal, pair with non-starchy vegetables, and prefer the cooled version where resistant starch (RS3) formation further reduces the effective glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat content (0.8g/100g; ~1.4g per piece) and absence of trans fat; moderate sodium is the primary cardiovascular concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ba-wan is relatively low in saturated fat (1.4g per piece) and contains no trans fat, making it a lower-risk choice for cardiovascular disease compared to fried or pastry-based dumplings; however, the moderate sodium content (684mg per piece) warrants attention for individuals managing blood pressure as a cardiovascular risk factor.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very low saturated fat; dried shiitake lentinan has sub-therapeutic immunomodulatory properties at single-serving dose
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ba-wan is very low in saturated fat (0.8g/100g) and contains dried shiitake mushroom, which provides lentinan with theoretical immunomodulatory properties; however, clinical relevance at a single-serving dose is negligible, and the dish is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ba-wan (肉圓, ròu yuán) is a traditional Taiwanese meat dumpling originating from Changhua County in central Taiwan, with roots in Japanese colonial-era food scarcity (1930s–1940s). The dish consists of a thick, translucent, gelatinous wrapper made from sweet potato starch and rice flour (~100g), enclosing a savoury filling of minced pork, bamboo shoots, and dried shiitake mushroom (~80g), and is served topped with a sweet-savory fermented soybean sauce. One piece weighs approximately 180g and provides ~261 kcal, 9.9g protein, 5g fat, and 45g carbohydrate. The wrapper is naturally gluten-free (sweet potato starch + rice flour) and gives ba-wan its characteristic chewy, sticky, semi-transparent appearance. Ba-wan is traditionally prepared by steaming or deep-frying (Changhua style) and is a staple of Taiwanese night markets and temple fairs. Nutritionally, ba-wan has a low GI (~52) due to the gelatinous starch matrix physically resisting amylase digestion, though the glycaemic load per piece is medium (GL ~23) due to the large carbohydrate portion. Sodium is moderate at ~684mg per piece, primarily from the soy-based filling and topping sauce. The dish is NOT halal (contains pork) and is dairy-free. Cooled ba-wan develops resistant starch (RS3), further lowering the effective glycaemic response. Key nutrients per 100g: 145 kcal, 5.5g protein, 2.8g fat, 0.8g saturated fat, 25g carbohydrate, 3.5g sugar, 0.8g dietary fibre, 380mg sodium, 95mg potassium, 0.5mg iron, 6mcg selenium.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for ba-wan as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component ingredients (sweet potato starch, ground pork, bamboo shoots, dried shiitake) weighted by recipe proportions, cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. GI is an analogous estimate based on sweet potato starch GI data from a peer-reviewed source (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The input record claimed HIGH confidence with FAO/INFOODS Taiwan + USDA sourcing; confidence is set to MEDIUM here because no single approved-source entry covers the composite dish directly.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for ba-wan as a composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables as of 2026. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish as served is not reported in available approved sources. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): sweet potato starch wrapper and pork filling prepared without hydrogenated fats; USDA FoodData Central reports 0g trans fat for all component ingredients. 3. GI of ~52 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement of ba-wan has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Direct measurement is recommended for clinical applications. 4. Glycaemic load per serving = (52 × 25.0 × 1.8) / 100 = 23.4, rounded to 23.0 (MEDIUM GL). 5. Ba-wan is NOT halal (contains pork) and is not suitable for Muslim dietary requirements. 6. The wrapper is gluten-free (sweet potato starch + rice flour only); individuals with coeliac disease should verify that no cross-contamination occurs in preparation. 7. Cooled ba-wan develops RS3 resistant starch, which further lowers the effective glycaemic response compared to freshly prepared hot ba-wan. 8. Ontology: No FoodOn URI or FoodEx2 code exists for ba-wan; closest_parent matches documented with gap notes. No SNOMED CT code identified. 9. This is a Taiwanese dish; multilingual naming requirements apply for Chinese script (satisfied: 肉圓 / 肉圆). No Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese multilingual requirements apply.
